file_name: A et al. - 2024 - A novel CNN framework for skin disease classification using adaptive percentage filter for image bin.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom-built Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for skin disease classification. It processes 10,015 dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset using an Adaptive Percentage Filter for Binarization (APFB) and inpainting for preprocessing, then feeds them into CNN models to classify seven types of skin diseases, including melanoma. The best model achieved 100% testing accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel CNN framework and an Adaptive Percentage Filter for Binarization (APFB). It details the methodology of processing 10,015 dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset, compares the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques (CLAHE, Bilateral), and reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 100% testing accuracy) in Sections 3 and 4, which is characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: A novel CNN framework for skin disease classification using adaptive percentage filter for image binarization and fast-marching inpainting method
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin disease classification by proposing a novel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework that incorporates an Adaptive Percentage Filter for Binarization (APFB) to remove hairline noise from dermoscopic images. The researchers analyzed 10,015 images from the HAM10000 dataset, which contains seven diagnostic categories of pigmented lesions including melanoma. The APFB method was evaluated against state-of-the-art noise removal techniques like CLAHE and Bilateral filters using metrics such as PSNR, SSIM, BRISQUE, and NIQE, demonstrating superior image quality. The preprocessed images were then fed into custom-built CNN models to classify the skin diseases based on features like pigment, texture, and symmetry. The proposed APFB-CNN model achieved a testing accuracy of 100%, outperforming other models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-measure. The results suggest that this non-invasive AI-based approach can effectively predict skin diseases, potentially reducing the need for invasive biopsies.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions acquired from the Kaggle repository. The dataset contains images confirmed with histopathology by board-certified dermatologists.
classes_used: Actinic Keratoses And Intraepithelial Carcinoma / Bowen’s Disease (akiec)

use.training: 70% of the pre-processed images were used for training the custom-built CNN models.
use.testing: 30% of the pre-processed images were used for testing the model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing; it compared methods using descriptive metrics (PSNR, SSIM, BRISQUE, NIQE, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-measure) without statistical significance tests.

hairline_noise_removal_metrics.description: Comparison of APFB against CLAHE and Bilateral methods using PSNR, BRISQUE, SSIM, and NIQE metrics.
hairline_noise_removal_metrics.notes: APFB method showed superior image quality preservation compared to CLAHE and Bilateral methods.

skin_disease_classification.description: Classification of seven skin disease categories using custom-built CNN models. 70% of images used for training, 30% for testing. Optimizer: Adam, Learning Rate: 0.01, Epochs: 10, Batch Size: 30.

O-CNN.description: Model using original images with hairline noise.
O-CNN.testing_accuracy: Lower than APFB-CNN (exact value not explicitly stated in text, but implied to be inferior)

CLAHE-CNN.testing_accuracy: Lower than APFB-CNN (exact value not explicitly stated in text, but implied to be inferior)

Bilateral-CNN.testing_accuracy: Lower than APFB-CNN (exact value not explicitly stated in text, but implied to be inferior)

APFB-CNN.notes: Best performing model among the four CNN models created.

skin_disease_classification.notes: The APFB-CNN model outperformed other models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-measure. The paper claims the application can identify conditions with 99% Sensitivity and Specificity in the introduction, but the specific experimental result for the proposed model is 100% testing accuracy.

architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 used for pre-processing to extract features (as mentioned in Section 3 and Conclusion), though the final classification is performed by the custom CNN.

layer_specifications.activation: Softmax (implied by categorical cross-entropy and multi-class classification)

preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Percentage Filter for Binarization (APFB) for hairline noise detection
Fast-marching inpainting method (Alexandru Telea’s) for hairline noise removal
VGG16 pre-processing for feature extraction (pigments, colors, shapes, symmetries)
CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) for hairline noise removal (used for comparison)
Bilateral filter for hairline noise removal (used for comparison)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions that data augmentation techniques were applied as part of pre-processing in the context of related work (specifically citing Fu'adah et al. [17] who employed augmentation techniques and reshaping images). However, the specific augmentation methods, probabilities, or settings used in the current study's proposed APFB-CNN framework are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed Adaptive Percentage Filter for Binarization (APFB) combined with fast-marching inpainting effectively removes hairline noise from dermoscopic images while preserving structural, luminance, and contrast integrity.
The APFB method demonstrated superior image quality compared to state-of-the-art methods (CLAHE and Bilateral filters) based on PSNR, SSIM, BRISQUE, and NIQE metrics.

The custom-built CNN model utilizing APFB preprocessed images (APFB-CNN) achieved 100% testing accuracy, outperforming models using original images (O-CNN), CLAHE preprocessed images (CLAHE-CNN), and Bilateral preprocessed images (Bilateral-CNN).
The APFB-CNN model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-measure (all equal to 1) on the test set for the HAM10000 dataset.
The study suggests that the proposed non-invasive AI-based approach can accurately predict skin diseases, including melanoma, potentially reducing the need for invasive biopsies.
The authors acknowledge that future research should explore variations in epochs, batch sizes, classifiers, and optimizers to further refine and validate the approach.
important_remarks: The reported 100% testing accuracy for the APFB-CNN model is highly suspicious and likely indicates data leakage, as achieving perfect classification on a diverse, multi-source dataset like HAM10000 is statistically improbable without leakage or overfitting.
The study lacks external validation; results are derived solely from the HAM10000 dataset, limiting generalizability to other clinical settings or image acquisition devices.
No statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) were reported to support the claimed superiority of the APFB-CNN model over baselines.

The paper claims 99% sensitivity and specificity in the introduction but reports 100% accuracy in results without providing a detailed breakdown of per-class sensitivity and specificity, creating a discrepancy in reported performance metrics.

The custom CNN architecture is relatively small (approx. 13 million parameters) compared to state-of-the-art deep learning models, raising questions about whether the high performance is due to the model's capacity or preprocessing artifacts.

The dataset is heavily imbalanced (e.g., 6,705 Melanocytic Nevi vs. 115 DermatoFibroma), yet the paper does not explicitly detail how class imbalance was handled during training (e.g., weighted loss, oversampling), which could bias the 100% accuracy result towards majority classes.

The claim that the model can replace invasive biopsies is clinically overstated given the lack of prospective clinical trials, real-world deployment data, or comparison against ground-truth histopathology in a live clinical workflow.
